statusLine: EU fuel stocks sit in 'watch' territory as of March 2026, with 14 of 27 member states holding at least one fuel type below the mandatory 90-day minimum, and jet fuel emerging as the most widespread vulnerability across the bloc.
fullAnalysis:
As of March 2026, the EU's aggregate oil stock position remains under pressure, with bloc-wide averages of 95.6 days for petrol, 88.1 days for diesel, and 87.0 days for jet fuel. While petrol sits marginally above the mandatory 90-day minimum, both diesel and jet fuel fall short at the aggregate level — a meaningful signal given that diesel underpins road freight and industrial logistics across the continent, and jet fuel supports aviation recovery. The overall 'watch' classification reflects a situation that is not yet an emergency but leaves limited buffer against further supply disruptions. Brent crude has climbed 9.7% since late February 2026 — from $72.48 to $79.49 per barrel — a rise consistent with the strategic reserve releases several member states have undertaken in response to Middle East supply disruptions, which have drawn down stockpiles that would otherwise provide that buffer.

At the country level, the picture is considerably more uneven. Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, and Portugal each register critical status across all three fuel categories, with some figures well below even half the 90-day threshold. Romania's diesel stands at just 30.2 days — the lowest single reading in the dataset — while Portugal and Poland both record jet fuel stocks below 22 days. These are not marginal shortfalls; at current consumption rates, these countries would exhaust emergency-equivalent reserves within weeks absent resupply. Italy, France, Lithuania, Czechia, and Croatia also show critical readings across multiple fuel types, meaning that 10 of 27 countries are critical in two or more categories simultaneously.

Jet fuel is the most structurally exposed product in this dataset. Of the 27 member states, 18 show jet fuel stocks at critical status, with only Ireland, Croatia, Slovenia, Estonia, and Sweden holding safe or watch-level positions. This is particularly notable given the summer travel season: June–August typically represents peak aviation demand across Europe, meaning drawdowns will accelerate precisely when stocks are already thin. Countries such as Finland (31.9 days), Portugal (21.2 days), and Poland (22.5 days) face acute near-term pressure in this category. By contrast, diesel reserves show more variation — the Netherlands holds 207.5 days and Finland 197.7 days, providing significant national buffers — though these surpluses are not easily or quickly redistributed across borders under emergency conditions.

On fuel prices, the EU average as of 15 June 2026 stands at €1.771/L for petrol and €1.781/L for diesel. The price spread between the most and least expensive member states remains wide: Danish consumers pay €2.392/L for petrol against €1.34/L in Malta — a gap that partly reflects tax and subsidy structures rather than supply conditions alone. Poland, notably one of the most stock-constrained countries in the dataset, records some of the lowest pump prices at €1.408/L, which may reflect administered pricing or cross-border dynamics rather than genuine market equilibrium. Policymakers should monitor whether artificially suppressed prices in low-stock countries are compounding demand pressure on already depleted reserves.
keyPoints: EU diesel (88.1 days) and jet fuel (87.0 days) averages sit below the mandatory 90-day minimum as of March 2026, while petrol (95.6 days) marginally exceeds it.

18 of 27 member states are at critical status for jet fuel, with Portugal (21.2 days), Poland (22.5 days), and Finland (31.9 days) the most exposed — a serious concern entering peak summer aviation season.
Romania's diesel stock of 30.2 days is the single lowest reading across all countries and fuel types, representing less than one third of the required minimum.

Brent crude has risen 9.7% since late February 2026 to $79.49/barrel, reflecting ongoing Middle East supply disruptions that have prompted strategic reserve releases across multiple member states, further drawing down the stocks recorded here.

A wide price disparity persists across the bloc — petrol ranges from €1.34/L in Malta to €2.392/L in Denmark — and low pump prices in stock-constrained countries such as Poland may be masking or amplifying underlying supply pressure.
